EVENT_NARRATIVE: East Howard Street was flooded by a tributary of Vaughn Creek. Also, water was reported to be swiftly moving across a low part of Landrum Road, likely due to a creek (5 SE Columbus). An NWS employee reported 4.35 inches of rain in this area in two hours (4 E Columbus).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances tracked across central North Carolina and interacted with a warm moist air mass and produced isolated wind damage and an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down approximately 10 miles northwest of Roxboro, North Carolina. Although the tornado was only on the ground for two minutes significant tree and structure damage occurred, including one covered boat dock on Hyco Lake that was destroyed. Moderate damage to several homes and other nearby covered boat docks was reported, including damage to roofing material on several homes and a multi-story balcony that was uplifted and damaged on one home. Dozens of trees along the damage path were snapped and uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances tracked across central North Carolina and interacted with a warm moist air mass and produced isolated wind damage and an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees, a fence and a shed were reported blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances tracked across central North Carolina and interacted with a warm moist air mass and produced isolated wind damage and an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 20 trees were reported down, along with an outbuilding flipped over and some flashing blown off a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances tracked across central North Carolina and interacted with a warm moist air mass and produced isolated wind damage and an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down in the road at the intersection of North Carolina Highway 87 and Spring Valley Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A solid band of strong to severe showers and storms formed along an old outflow across north central NC on Sunday, September 1st, 2013. These storms became aligned roughly along I-40 and Highway 64, and tended to train repeatedly over the same areas. They knocked down trees, caused generally minor structural damage, produced flooding along|Crabtree Creek and generated a lot of CG lightning over a populated area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Odyssey Drive was closed near the 1300 block due to two feet of water flowing across the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The line of thunderstorms that developed in advance of a slow moving frontal boundary across central and eastern Virginia continued to develop southwestward during the late evening hours and eventually into north central North Carolina. With a very moist and unstable air mass present across the area, the thunderstorms quickly intensified to strong to severe levels producing damaging wind gusts in Caswell county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Caswell County 911 Center reported that a tree was down on Roxboro Lake Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary to warm front remained across the southern half of Virginia westward into southern West Virginia.  A warm, very moist, unstable air mass persisted across much of the region south of the front.  Isolated to scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed once again across the region, mainly across Southside Virginia and the Piedmont of north central North Carolina.  One of these in Caswell county became severe blowing down a few trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Caswell County 911 Center reported that a tree was blown down by thunderstorm winds along U.S. Highway 158 West.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass and southeast, upslope flow helped to support localized very heavy rain over parts of the North Carolina mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cane Creek Road in the northeast part of the county was flooded. Water was reported over the road along 226 N in the northern part of the country. Sparks Road was reportedly flooded during the late afternoon. Heavy rain continued into the evening and overnight hours with more roads flooded as the event transitioned from a short fused flash flood event to one of duration flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house and started a fire in Wilmington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home on 111 Pitch Pine Court, starting a fire. All indications are that significant damage was done before fire crew could put out the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very weak upper trough persisted across the region on the east side of a strong subtropical ridge that continued to expand through the central and southeastern states.  A very weak diffuse cold front lingered through the region, but was rapidly washing out.  The air mass across the region had become increasingly moist and unstable.  While atmospheric dynamics were weak, sufficient lift, moisture, and instability were present for scattered pop-up pulse thunderstorms to develop across the region, especially Southside Virginia and north central North Carolina.  A few of these became severe producing damaging wind gusts and non-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stokes County 911 Center reported that a couple of power lines and two trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds in the Sandy Ridge area.

Two trees were down five miles east-northeast of Walnut Grove, one on Pole Bridge Road and another on Gibson Road with another tree down on River Road near Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms blew down a few trees over the North Carolina foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Cove Rd, about 10 miles northwest of Rutherfordton. Another tree was blown down nearby on Owens Chapel Rd near Union Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable northwest flow aloft persisted across the region thanks to an equally persistent strong subtropical ridge across the central U.S.  A strengthening upper level storm system was diving southeast from the Great Lakes.  The air mass across the region was unseasonably warm and unstable for late August.  These factors combined to produce isolated severe thunderstorms.  The initial thunderstorms developed across far southwest Virginia and southeast West Virginia during the early to mid-afternoon as a result of outflow boundaries from overnight thunderstorms that moved through Kentucky, Tennessee and western West Virginia.  The thunderstorms propagated and developed eastward through the evening, impacting much of the Blacksburg, VA County Warning Area (CWA), including the Piedmont of Virginia and North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Employees of the Mount Airy Post Office observed that several trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds along North Carolina Route 601 just south of Mount Airy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorms that developed in far southern Virginia during the late evening hours of the 9th propagated into far northwest North Carolina after midnight and continued into the early morning hours of the 10th.  This resulted in rainfall of two to three inches in a couple of hours.  The ground in this mountainous region of northwest North Carolina was very saturated from excessive rainfall all summer.||During the late morning into the early afternoon, thunderstorms redeveloped in southwest Virginia.  This activity increased and expanded in aerial coverage as it drifted southeast into northwest North Carolina during the mid to late afternoon.  As a result, additional flooding was reported in two separate locations in Wilkes county during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pond Mountain Fire Department reported that a family had to be rescued from their home on Flatwoods School Road when Big Laurel Creek Flooded the area.  Rainfall up to two inches had fallen in northwest Ashe county since midnight.  The mountains of northwest North Carolina had consistently received excessive rainfall all summer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped across southern West Virginia into central Virginia.  A broad upper-level low persisted across the northeast United States and southeast Canada while a broad subtropical ridge remained in place across the southern United States.  Very moist tropical air with precipitable water values near 2.0 inches persisted across southern Virginia and northern North Carolina.  With daytime heating, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon from near Martinsville, Virginia southwestward into western North Carolina producing damaging winds gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was down by thunderstorm winds along Rome Anthony Road near the intersection of Lone Hickory Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across the region triggered widespread rain showers ahead of the boundary, with little to no lightning observed.  Early morning showers and cloud cover strictly limited heating, resulting in low CAPE values.  The main line of convection passed across the mountains during early afternoon, triggering winds in the 25 to 35 mph range per weather spotter reports.  However, as the line passed east of the Blue Ridge, it encountered a stronger low level jet and higher shear and helicity, which helped the convection to rapidly intensify.  Strong wind reports remained spotty along the main line, however several subtle couplets of rotation were noted on radar along the line in northern North Carolina and southern Virginia. One of these couplets produced a brief tornado in Stokes County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down on a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a southward moving cold front. One fast moving multicell severe storm caused wind damage over the southern foothills of North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of pennies was reported in the Mill Spring community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a southward moving cold front. One fast moving multicell severe storm caused wind damage over the southern foothills of North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down on a home on Red Fox Rd, about 5 miles east northeast of Tryon. A tree was blown down on power lines on Golf Course Rd near Landrum Rd. A fell on a home Collinsville Rd, about 6 miles east southeast of Columbus. A shed was also flipped in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in advance of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on Gum Spring Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed over the region ahead of a cold front. The storms produced a mix of hail and damaging winds across western North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Landrum Rd. Several large limbs were blown down nearby on Woody Circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ferocious line of strong to severe thunderstorms raced through central North Carolina|during the afternoon and into the early evening, uprooting trees and snapping power lines with straight-line wind gusts up to 80 mph. Damage was widespread with numerous trees falling on homes throughout the county warning area. The city of Albermarle, in Stanly County was hardest hit, with approximately 70 homes damaged and at least 3 injuries from trees crashing into and through homes and mobile homes. A University of North Carolina student   also died from injuries received when a large tree fell and landed on top of her on the sidewalk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread trees and power-lines were blown down throughout the county. Northwestern portions of the county were the hardest hit.  Monetary damages were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The airmass was unstable ahead of a cold front. A mid-level shortwave trough embedded in west-southwest flow moved across the area during the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a house on Pine Street. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused some minor street flooding in southeastern North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covering the road at the intersection of Hwy 421 and Hwy 210.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed eastern North Carolina during the early morning hours of January 31st, 2013. A strong low level jet accompanied the storms and although no thunder was reported, there were several reports of strong wind gusts with damage to trees, powerlines, and a few structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Bern fire officials reported a tree fell on a car on Pelican Drive in Fairfield Harbour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea tracked northeast up the East Coast and through North Carolina on June 7th, bringing a swath of heavy rain of 3 to 6 inches across central and eastern portions of the area. This heavy rain resulted in numerous road closures and brought several small creeks and streams out of their banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crabtree Creek rose out of its bank causing flooding along Old Wake Forest Road, Highway 70 and Blue Ridge Road. Cars parked at the Crabtree Valley Mall had to be towed out of flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over eastern Tennessee ahead of a cold front. The line pushed east across the mountains and then across the foothills and piedmont during the afternoon and early evening hours. The line formed in an airmass characterized by high instability and moderate wind shear, and several of the storms produced areas of straight-line wind damage and one rare tornado in the North Carolina Mountains. The storms also produced large hail over the foothills and piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported along highway 321 near Radio St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The forecast area remained sandwiched between an unseasonably deep trough aloft to the west and a ridge to the east. This resulted in a persistent very moist air mass and with soils already saturated, two rounds of heavy rain resulted in flash flooding, first in the early morning hours across Durham and Orange counties and later in the day across the same area, with additional flooding in Chatham, Anson and Stanly counties. Chapel Hill and Carrboro experienced the most significant flooding, with substantial property damage occurring. In addition, as afternoon destabilization occurred, a couple of storms became severe and produced some isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs were blown down in the Durham area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over eastern Tennessee ahead of a cold front. The line pushed east across the mountains and then across the foothills and piedmont during the afternoon and early evening hours. The line formed in an airmass characterized by high instability and moderate wind shear, and several of the storms produced areas of straight-line wind damage and one rare tornado in the North Carolina Mountains. The storms also produced large hail over the foothills and piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were blown down from about 4 miles west northwest of Morganton, across the north and east side of Morganton and across Drexel. The Drexel and Valdese areas were hardest hit, with several roads blocked by trees. One tree fell on a house on Laurel St NE, about a mile north of Valdese. A 55 mph wind gust was measured at the Hickory Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered slow moving thunderstorms developed along and ahead of cold front. Some of the storms produced damaging wind. There was also an area of urban flash flooding near Charlotte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down in the area around South Park Mall, with one tree on a house on Scofield Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms repeatedly moved across the same areas over the North Carolina foothills, causing a high impact flash flood event over Catawba, Lincoln and Cleveland counties. In Catawba County alone, between 50 and 60 roads were closed at the height of the event. Several roads were heavily damaged and would take months to repair. Over 400 structures sustained some amount of damage in the county, with 72 heavily damaged or destroyed. A trained spotter in western Catawba County reported 11.31 of rain in a four hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parts of Island Ford Road were flooded by streams between Old Mountain Road and Sharon School Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of severe thunderstorms within lines of heavy rain showers moved from Virginia into North Carolina. The severe storms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds increased across central North Carolina ahead of a high amplitude trough aloft and a strong surface front. With an unseasonably warm and moist air mass in place, a squall line developed ahead of the approaching front, further enhancing the winds and causing wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down along a swath from just southwest of Walnut Creek to just northeast of Walnut Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture, combined with a nearly stationary frontal boundary produced flooding over southeast North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on White Road.  Smaller vehicles could not pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving line of thunderstorms crossed all of western North Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours. The storms developed along a strong cold front. Despite their slow movement, the storms were able to mix down strong environmental winds, causing areas of straight line wind damage, especially in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down across the county as a line of heavy rain showers with embedded thunderstorms moved over the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds aloft were in place well ahead of a cold front. There were many reports of gradient wind damage through the day and into the night. A line of forced convection developed along the front and swept across the area during the late night hours. The convection slightly enhanced the strong winds and resulted in additional reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Butler Farm Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplifying upper level trough and a nearly stationary front across the Coastal Plain resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms. Some isolated flash flooding occurred as a few of the storms were nearly stationary and antecedent ground conditions were already wet due to several days of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Main Street in Wilsons Mills had to closed for several hours due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low slowly lifted northeast across the southern Appalachian Mountains over the 3 day period. While precipitable water values were not particularly strong with the low, a strong low level jet in advance of the system brought strong moisture transport and orographic ascent to the region, centered over the Blue Ridge Mountains of North Carolina. The hardest hit areas saw between 4 and 7 inches of rain over a two day period, resulting in a widespread duration flood event. Mainstem rivers and reservoirs were also significantly affected by prolonged period of heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two mudslides were reported on highway 74 in Gerton. A mudslide was also reported on Middlefork Rd off of highway 74. The roads were closed for a while.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the region in the form of a squall line of storms. Winds of 60 to 70 mph accompanied this squall line, resulting a widespread wind damage. Large hail also accompanied some of the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds along a squall line downed trees in Boone. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure and its associated cold front moved across the area and produced a shallow line of showers. These showers tapped 50 to 70 knot winds at 3000 to 4000 feet AGL and brought them to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey team concluded a microburst with winds to 75 mph did considerable damage to a small mobile home community. There were approximately 12 mobile homes that experienced damage with one home sustaining major damage. Two pickup trucks were damaged by fallen trees. A dozen sheds and storage buildings were damaged or destroyed. Many windows were broken by the severe winds and debris was strewn across a wide area. A dozen trees were downed or snapped and many large limbs were down. Power lines were also reported down and seven hundred people were reportedly without power. There were two wind maxima associated with this microburst. A 70 mph wind gust caused damage along Joe Brown Highway, just south of McMillan Road. A 75 mph wind gust caused damage in the vicinity of Best Road, just east of Joe Brown Highway. The microburst dissipated north of Best Road, but well south of Old Lumberton Road. The path width was 1936 yards and the path length was 1.15 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep, full-latitude upper trough was sweeping from the central U.S. into the eastern U.S. during this period.  As with the event exactly one week before, a warm, moist air mass was present across the region in advance of this front.  Once again, a strong line of thunderstorms, or quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) formed in advance of the cold front associated with the upper low, moving through the region during peak heating time, namely afternoon and early evening.  Some discrete strong cells were also noted in advance of the main thunderstorm line, but many of these managed to remain just east or south of the Blacksburg County Warning Area (CWA).  As with the event on April 12th, the Piedmont region of Southside Virginia and north central North Carolina was the hardest hit.  Dozens to hundreds of trees were blown down by strong thunderstorm winds gusts from Stokes county on the west end to Caswell county on the east end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stokes County Emergency Manager reported that there were considerable damage to trees in and around the town of Kind.  The Stokes County 911 Center reported that numerous trees and large tree limbs were down in and around the town of King.  A tree was down on a building near Main Street causing $70,000 in damage alone to the structure and vehicles parked inside.  Trees and/or large branches were also down on West Street, Spruce Street, and on Redwood Bend Road just outside of town.  In Walnut Cove, further east, a tree was blown down on a church as reported by local broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving storms produced heavy rain and sporatic flooding over a large portion of southeastern North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Broad Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The next event of hydrologic significance in July took place as a strong (for late July) cold front approached the southern Appalachians on the 27th. Rapid moisture return ahead of the front set the stage for widespread showers and thunderstorms across the mountains and eventually spreading into the piedmont. Pre-frontal rainfall broke out early on the 27th as storms took the preferred July 2013 track through the northwest NC mountain counties into southwest Virginia. Repeated bouts of convection and heavy rainfall affected much of this area during the day of the 27th bringing impressive 48-hour gage reports from this area ending at 12Z on the 28th. The top five included Sandy Flats IFLOWS (SDRN7) 7.53���, Wilbar RG DCP (WLBN7) 6.07���, Triplett RG DCP (TPTN7) 5.22���, Parkway School IFLOWS (EKCN7) 4.25���, and Jefferson 2E COOP (JFFN7) 3.48���.  In Watauga, Wilkes, and Ashe counties there were several reports of flooded roadways and stranded motorists being rescued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Silas Creek was flooding along Mouth of Silas Creek Road and a several people were reported to be stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms associated with a cold front erupted during the afternoon mainly over the piedmont. A few of the storms became severe with large hail and severe level winds.
A tropical-like airmass produced heavy rain with street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported with stalled cars near New Hanover High School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in advance of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported across the intersection of South Main Street and Kelly Street. The time was estimated based on radar data.
